skill.name: browser-act-skill-forge
skill.description: Explores websites via browser-act automation and packages discoveries into callable Skill packages (SKILL.md + scripts) — execute reliably and efficiently without re-exploration. Use when: user wants a reusable Skill for any website ('create/make/build a skill for X'), needs to understand a site's internal APIs ('what endpoint does X use', 'how does X load data'), or requires batch extraction at scale (hundreds+ records — forge a Skill first, then execute reliably). Differs from browser-act: produces reusable packages, not one-off execution. Triggers: 'explore API behind X', 'what endpoint does X use', 'create/forge a skill for [site]', 'build a tool for [site]', 'extract/scrape/crawl N items at scale', 'automate this permanently', 'make this reusable', 'too slow/fragile — package it', 'explore website internals', 'every day/week I have to...', 'monitor [site] for changes', 'browser-act-skill-forge'. Simulated human-operation automation within user's authenticated session.
skill.summary: Explores websites via browser automation and packages API and DOM discoveries into reusable, callable Skill packages for reliable reuse without re-exploration.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: After evaluating 407 static analysis findings across 4 files (1219 lines), ALL findings are determined to be FALSE POSITIVES. The scanner flagged Ruby shell backtick execution (309), weak cryptographic algorithms (88), and network calls (10) — all of which appear exclusively in markdown code blocks and documentation templates as example commands and placeholder content, not as executable code. This skill is a legitimate browser automation toolkit that explores websites and packages discoveries into reusable Skill packages. It transparently documents its purpose and operational boundaries. The verified risk factors (external_commands and network) are expected for a browser automation tool and are not indicative of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution in Documentation - FALSE POSITIVE (Dismissed)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 309 instances of Ruby shell backtick execution, shell command substitution, and template literal command substitution across all 4 files. All instances are in markdown code blocks showing example CLI commands (e.g., `network requests --type xhr,fetch --filter {domain keyword}`) and template Python invocation patterns (e.g., `eval "$(python scripts/xxx.py {params})"`). These are documentation examples and template code, not actively executed commands. No user input is injected into shell commands at runtime through these patterns. Confidence: 0.98. Confidence reasoning: All occurrences are inside markdown code fences or inline code formatting — the scanner pattern-matched backtick characters without context.

medium_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm References in Templates - FALSE POSITIVE (Dismissed)
medium_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 88 instances of weak cryptographic algorithm patterns. All instances are template placeholder values inside markdown code blocks showing example JSON output structures (e.g., `{"field-name": "example-value"}`) and example JavaScript snippets with placeholder variables like `b.opts`, `el.val`. These are not actual cryptographic implementations. The scanner likely matched variable names and placeholder content that happen to resemble cryptographic patterns. No actual weak crypto algorithms are used. Confidence: 0.99. Confidence reasoning: All 88 flagged instances are template mock data in code examples — no `MD5`, `SHA1`, `DES`, `RC4`, or similar actual weak crypto implementations exist in any file.

low_findings.title: Network Calls in Documentation Examples - FALSE POSITIVE (Dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 10 network-related patterns (Fetch API calls and hardcoded URLs). All instances are in markdown code blocks showing example API exploration commands (e.g., `fetch('https://example.com/api/v1/items')`) and template navigation steps. The `https://www.browseract.com` URL in SKILL.md:7 is the legitimate project homepage. No actual network exfiltration or unauthorized data transmission is present. Confidence: 0.97. Confidence reasoning: All network patterns use placeholder URLs like `example.com` or reference the skill's own project homepage — there is no evidence of data being sent to unexpected destinations.

low_findings.title: System and Network Reconnaissance Patterns - FALSE POSITIVE (Dismissed)
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged DOM element access patterns (e.g., `element.shadowRoot.querySelector`, `state` element index access) as system/network reconnaissance. These are legitimate browser automation techniques for finding elements in Shadow DOM and referencing page elements by state index. The skill is designed to interact with web pages through the user's browser — DOM exploration is its core function, not reconnaissance. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: All flagged patterns are standard web automation/DOM manipulation techniques used by browser automation tools — they operate on the user's own browser session, not remote systems.

dangerous_patterns.title: Ruby Shell Backtick Execution in Markdown Code Blocks
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: Ruby/Shell backtick execution syntax (e.g., `command` inside markdown code fences). Verdict: FALSE POSITIVE. All 309 occurrences are in documentation markdown files showing example CLI commands. The backtick characters are markdown formatting, not Ruby execution. This is a well-known false positive pattern for static scanners analyzing markdown documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Python Invocation Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Pattern: `$(python scripts/xxx.py)` syntax in markdown code blocks suggesting command substitution. Verdict: FALSE POSITIVE. These are template invocation patterns in the output_template.md file showing how generated skills will run their Python scripts. The pattern is documentation of the intended usage, not active execution. The generated Python scripts only output JS strings via print() — they do not execute arbitrary commands.

content.user_title: Forge Reusable Browser Automation Skills from Any Website
content.value_statement: This skill solves the problem of repetitive website data extraction and automation. It explores websites to discover API endpoints and DOM structures, then packages them into reusable, callable Skill packages for one-click execution.
actual_capabilities: Discovers API endpoints behind websites by inspecting network traffic and HAR recordings
Creates reusable browser automation skills from any website without manual coding
Generates Python wrapper scripts that output executable JavaScript for browser eval
Automatically verifies generated skills through end-to-end testing in a sub-agent
Supports both data extraction and website operation automation through API or DOM
Packages skills with SKILL.md documentation and ready-to-run Python scripts
limitations: Cannot bypass authentication or access controls the user does not already have
Only operates on websites the user can access through their browser session
Generated skills may break if target websites change their API endpoints or DOM structure
Exploration is limited to 100 tool call steps before asking the user for guidance

use_cases.title: Data Analyst Extracting Product Catalogs
use_cases.description: A data analyst needs to extract product listings, prices, and reviews from an e-commerce site for competitive analysis. This skill creates a reusable extraction tool that can be invoked on demand.

use_cases.title: Developer Building Workflow Automations
use_cases.description: A developer wants to automate repetitive form submissions or data entry tasks on internal web applications. This skill captures the workflow and generates a repeatable automation package.

use_cases.title: Operations Professional Monitoring Websites
use_cases.description: An operations professional needs to regularly monitor competitor pricing or website content changes. This skill forges a monitoring tool that can be executed periodically without re-exploration.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Data Extraction from a Simple Website
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a skill that extracts the latest articles from news.ycombinator.com
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to extract data from a simple list-based website

prompt_templates.title: API Discovery Behind a Website
prompt_templates.prompt: Explore the API endpoints behind twitter.com and create a skill to search for tweets by keyword
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to programmatically access a website's internal API

prompt_templates.title: Operation Automation for Form Submission
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a skill that fills out a contact form on example.com and submits it with specified parameters
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to automate website operations like form submission

prompt_templates.title: Large-Scale Batch Extraction with Pagination
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a skill to extract all product listings from amazon.com with pagination, filtering by category and sorting by price
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to extract large amounts of data with multiple parameters and pagination

output_examples.input: Extract the latest articles from news.ycombinator.com
output_examples.output: The skill explores the Hacker News front page, discovers the Algolia search API endpoint from network traffic, and generates a reusable skill package at output/hackernews-list-articles/. The package includes a SKILL.md with usage instructions and a Python script that accepts parameters for filtering by date or keyword.

output_examples.input: Monitor price changes on an e-commerce product page
output_examples.output: The skill navigates to the product page, identifies the product data API endpoint from network traffic, verifies parameter injection via URL navigation, and produces a skill that can be invoked daily to check the current price. All exploration data and extraction results stay on the local machine.

output_examples.input: Create a skill to search and filter on a job portal
output_examples.output: Browser-act-skill-forge opens the job portal, observes network requests during a sample search, identifies the search API parameters, and generates a skill with Python scripts and a SKILL.md. The generated skill includes support for keyword, location, and category filters.

best_practices: Always let the skill explore API endpoints first before falling back to DOM extraction — API access is faster and more reliable for repeated use.
Test generated skills with 1-2 sample items before running large-scale batch extraction to verify correctness.
Use the skill forge before starting bulk data collection to avoid fragile one-off scraping attempts that fail on pagination or rate limits.
anti_patterns: Do not skip the exploration phase and attempt direct bulk scraping — this often leads to failures from anti-bot detection and pagination edge cases.
Do not modify generated Python scripts unless troubleshooting execution failures — they are verified during generation and should remain stable.
Do not attempt to access data beyond what is visible in your authenticated browser session — this skill respects access controls and cannot bypass them.

faq.question: What types of websites can this skill work with?
faq.answer: Any website accessible through a standard browser. The skill discovers API endpoints from network traffic and falls back to DOM extraction when APIs are not available.

faq.question: Does this skill bypass website authentication?
faq.answer: No. It operates within your authenticated browser session and can only access data you already have permission to view.

faq.question: Where are the generated skills stored?
faq.answer: Generated skills are stored in the output directory specified during creation, by default under the output folder in your working directory.

faq.question: Can I reuse a generated skill multiple times?
faq.answer: Yes. The main purpose of this skill is to create reusable packages that can be invoked later without repeating the exploration phase.

faq.question: Is my data sent to external servers?
faq.answer: No. All exploration data and extraction results stay on your local machine. The skill only communicates with the target websites you specify.

faq.question: What happens if a website changes its structure?
faq.answer: Generated skills may break if the target website changes its API endpoints or DOM structure. Run the skill forge again to rediscover and regenerate the skill.
